epigraph.text: I will multiply your offspring as the stars of heaven.
epigraph.quote: The exponential function is the most important function in mathematics.

content.text:
The exponential function extends from $\R$ to $\C$ in a natural way, 
but its behavior becomes far richer. The key to everything is Euler's 
formula.

content.title: Complex Exponential
content.content:
For $z = x + iy$, we define
\[
 e^z = e^x(\cos y + i\sin y).
\]

content.text:
This definition ensures that $e^z$ agrees with the real exponential when 
$z$ is real (since $\cos 0 + i\sin 0 = 1$) and satisfies the fundamental 
property $e^{z_1 + z_2} = e^{z_1}e^{z_2}$.

This can be proven rigorously using power series (which we develop in 
Chapter 6) or taken as the definition of $e^{i\theta}$. Either way, the 
result is consistent and leads to the same rich theory.

content.title: Properties of $e^z$
content.content:
For all $z, w \in \C$:

1. $e^z \neq 0$ for all $z$

2. $e^{z + w} = e^z e^w$

3. $e^{-z} = 1/e^z$

4. $|e^z| = e^{\re(z)} = e^x$

5. $\arg(e^z) = \im(z) = y$ (modulo $2\pi$)

6. $e^z$ is entire with $(e^z)' = e^z$

7. $e^z = 1$ if and only if $z = 2\pi ki$ for some integer $k$
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
For (iv): $|e^z| = |e^x||(\cos y + i\sin y)| = e^x \cdot 1 = e^x$.

For (vii): $e^z = e^x(\cos y + i\sin y) = 1$ requires $e^x = 1$ (so $x = 0$) 
and $\cos y + i\sin y = 1$ (so $y = 2\pi k$).

content.title: Periodicity of $e^z$
content.content:
The function $e^z$ is periodic with period $2\pi i$:
\[
 e^{z + 2\pi i} = e^z \quad \text{for all } z \in \C.
\]
This is the *only* period (up to integer multiples).
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$e^{z + 2\pi i} = e^z \cdot e^{2\pi i} = e^z \cdot 1 = e^z$.

If $e^{z + w} = e^z$ for all $z$, then $e^w = 1$, so $w = 2\pi ki$.

content.text:
The periodicity of $e^z$ is purely imaginary—the function is periodic 
in the vertical direction but unbounded horizontally. Each horizontal 
strip of width $2\pi$ (e.g., $0 \leq \im(z) < 2\pi$) maps onto 
$\C \setminus \{0\}$.

marginNotes.text:
And God said, "Let there be light," and 
there was light.

marginNotes.author: Leonhard Euler (1748)
marginNotes.text:
The most remarkable formula in 
mathematics.
